topic: An adult child moves back in with their parents
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the key turning in the lock was the breaking point. I stood in the doorway, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame, my breath catching in a ragged, shallow gasp. They were already settled, the easy, comfortable rhythm of their domesticity humming in the air, and I felt a violent tremor start deep in my chest. "I'm not staying," I managed, the words scraping out like broken glass. My voice was too high, too sharp, and I could feel the heat rising up my neck, staining my cheeks a furious crimson. I didn't wait for a reply; I spun on my heel, the sound of my boots echoing too loudly on the polished floor, and fled back into the suffocating silence of my car, the engine roaring a desperate protest.

[story 2]

I watched them from the hallway, a statue carved from pure, vibrating tension. Every casual touch, every murmured greeting, felt like a deliberate, calculated slight. The air in the living room seemed to thicken, pressing down on my lungs until each inhale was a conscious, painful effort. When my mother offered a gentle hand, I flinched back as if burned, my entire body rigid. "Don't," I hissed, the sound vibrating with an intensity that surprised even me. My jaw was clenched so tightly I tasted copper. I could see the flicker of confusion in their eyes, and it only fueled the burning sensation behind my ribs. I needed distance, absolute and immediate. I walked past them, my steps heavy and deliberate, each footfall a declaration of utter severance.

[story 3]

The sheer audacity of it made my vision swim. They had taken my space, my autonomy, and now they expected me to simply absorb it, to become part of their established landscape. I paced the length of the kitchen, my hands balled into fists so tight my nails bit into my palms. I slammed the refrigerator door shut with unnecessary force, the resulting thud shaking the glassware on the shelf. "This is ridiculous," I spat out, the words clipped and brittle. I felt a desperate need to dismantle everything they had built in an instant, to shatter the illusion of peace they were cultivating. My gaze swept over the room, landing on a framed photograph—a symbol of the life I felt I was being forced to rejoin—and I felt a profound, sickening surge of pure, unmanageable fury wash over me.
